ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for peanut-butter-filled sandwich biscuit; closest defensible match is the generic biscuit food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers the biscuits and crackers category; no specific sandwich-biscuit-with-peanut-filling code exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for peanut butter sandwich biscuit as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packet of peanut butter sandwich biscuits contains approximately 35 g (about 4 biscuits), providing roughly 180 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Sandwich Biscuit (Peanut Filling) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sandwich Biscuit (Peanut Filling), cr_id F11080058. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sweet Biscuits and Crackers — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for refined-flour sweet biscuit with sugar filling
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for peanut butter sandwich biscuit in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated from the closest analogous food: refined-flour sweet biscuit / cracker with sugar-based filling (GI range 65–75, high category). Glycemic load per 35 g serving = (70 × 60.16 × 0.35) / 100 = 14.7. This estimate is consistent with the input record's embedded GI value of 70 and the high-GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (414 mg/100 g) combined with high sugar (20.85 g/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.75 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid peanut butter sandwich biscuits; the 414 mg sodium per 100 g contributes substantially to daily sodium load, and the high sugar content (20.85 g/100 g) with minimal fibre (1.75 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention, compounding blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (70), high refined carbohydrate (60.16 g/100 g), high sugar (20.85 g/100 g), and low dietary fibre (1.75 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid peanut butter sandwich biscuits; the high GI of 70, combined with 60.16 g/100 g refined carbohydrate, 20.85 g/100 g sugar, and only 1.75 g/100 g dietary fibre, produces a rapid postprandial glucose spike that is not adequately offset by the 10.39 g/100 g peanut protein content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~7.5 g/100 g from palm oil), high sodium (414 mg/100 g), high sugar (20.85 g/100 g), and ultra-processed formulation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid peanut butter sandwich biscuits; the estimated 7.5 g/100 g saturated fat from palm oil exceeds the 5 g/100 g threshold, the 414 mg/100 g sodium raises blood pressure risk, and the ultra-processed refined-flour and sugar base contributes to dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peanut butter sandwich biscuit is an ultra-processed snack food consisting of two thin, crisp, baked refined-wheat-flour crackers sandwiching a sweet peanut butter-based filling. The biscuits are typically manufactured with palm oil, sugar, salt, and leavening agents, giving them a light, crunchy texture and a sweet-savoury flavour profile. They are widely sold in single-serve foil packets in Singapore school canteens, convenience stores, and supermarkets, and are popular as an affordable, shelf-stable snack for children and adults alike. Nutritionally, per 100 g the product delivers 514 kcal, 10.39 g protein, 24.66 g fat (approximately 7.5 g saturated from palm oil), 60.16 g carbohydrate, 20.85 g sugar, 1.75 g dietary fibre, and 414 mg sodium. The high GI of 70 and glycemic load of approximately 14.7 per 35 g serving make this food unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Despite the peanut protein content, the refined flour and sugar base dominates the glycaemic and inflammatory profile. Consumers seeking a peanut-based snack are better served by plain unsalted peanuts or peanut butter on wholegrain bread. In Singapore this snack is known in Chinese as 花生酱夹心饼干, in Malay as biskut sandwic isi kacang tanah, and is commonly found in school tuck shops and provision stores.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080058) with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database (refined-flour sweet biscuit, GI 70), consistent with the input embedded GI value. Saturated fat estimated at ~7.5 g/100 g based on typical palm-oil-based biscuit formulation; this is the only estimated value not directly reported by HPB. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided as this product is widely consumed in Singapore.
notes: iron_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database for this product). saturated_fat_g of 7.5 g/100 g is estimated from typical palm-oil-based biscuit formulation as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry; this value should be verified against product label when available. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific product are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (refined-flour sweet biscuit category); no direct measurement exists for this specific product. This food is classified as ultra-processed (NOVA Group 4). The food is widely consumed in Singapore as a school canteen and convenience snack. Multilingual names added in iteration 3 to satisfy G6 mandatory language requirements for a product widely consumed in Singapore: Chinese (花生酱夹心饼干 / 花生醬夾心餅乾), Malay (Biskut sandwic isi kacang tanah), Tamil (வேர்க்கடலை நிரப்பிய சாண்ட்விச் பிஸ்கட்), Hindi (मूंगफली भरावन सैंडविच बिस्कुट).
